strong: A: Sixth-century date — treats it as the pre-critical unanimous view and traces higher-critical rejection to anti-supernaturalist premises.
B: Fourth kingdom is Rome, with a still-future revived/final stage — confirmed by Revelation 13/17's ten kings.
C: Little horn is the future Antichrist — Antiochus IV permitted only as type, not fulfillment.
D: Gap theory of Daniel 9 — literal year-weeks, seventieth week still future, Antichrist confirms and breaks the covenant.
E: Dispensational-futurist reading of Daniel's apocalyptic — Revelation 6-19 is the parallel expansion.
F: Premillennial — the fifth kingdom of Dan 2/7 is a future political worldwide kingdom, not the church age.
H: Consistent futurist premillennialism — Dan 7's climax, Dan 9:27, Dan 11:36ff, and Dan 12:1 converge on one future end-time.
J: Son of Man is the divine messianic Christ, distinct from the Ancient of Days and from the saints.
K: Classical dispensationalism — three prophetic programs (nations/Israel/church), Daniel addresses nations and Israel.
L: Literal-historical-predictive hermeneutic ('normal sense') layered with recognition of apocalyptic symbol.
N: Tight cross-book coherence — Daniel 2/7/9/11-12 ↔ Matthew 24 ↔ 2 Thessalonians 2 ↔ Revelation 11-13, 17.
O: Single-fulfillment futurism for end-time details; Antiochus as type, not partial fulfillment.

positions.position: Sixth-century B.C. composition by Daniel himself; higher-critical second-century date rejected as anti-supernaturalist.
positions.rationale: Walvoord treats the sixth-century date as the unanimous view of the church until Porphyry's third-century attack, and notes that higher criticism only revived Porphyry's thesis as part of a broader denial of predictive prophecy and verbal-plenary inspiration. Conservative scholars, he says, have given 'almost universal recognition' to Daniel as a sixth-century work; the Maccabean date is an anti-Christian premise, not a new discovery of fact.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV4 — Date And Authorship

quote.text: Except for the attack of the pagan Porphyry (third century a.d.), no question was raised concerning the traditional sixth century b.c. date, the authorship of Daniel the prophet, or the genuineness of the book until the rise of higher criticism in the seventeenth century

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV4 — Date And Authorship

quote.text: conservative scholars have given almost universal recognition to the book of Daniel as an authentic sixth century b.c. composition of Daniel, the captive of Nebuchadnezzar

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV11 — Genuineness

quote.text: It should be noted at the outset (1) that the theory had an anti-Christian origination; (2) that no new facts had been determined to change the previous judgment of the church

positions.position: Rome, with a still-future revived/final stage in which ten contemporaneous kings arise and the little horn rules for three-and-a-half years before being destroyed at Christ's return.
positions.rationale: Walvoord holds the Roman identification as the consensus of conservative scholarship, rooted in Daniel 2 and 7 alike and confirmed by Revelation 13 and 17. Crucially, he insists the prophecy is not exhausted by historical Rome: the ten horns must be simultaneous kings at the end of the age, and the fourth kingdom has a 'yet future stage' in which the little horn reigns for the final three-and-a-half years before being destroyed by the coming Son of Man.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV76 — The Fourth Beast: Rome

quote.text: The crucial issue in the interpretation of the entire book of Daniel, and especially of chapter 7, is the identification of the fourth beast. On this point, liberal critics generally insist that the fourth beast is Greece or the kingdom of Alexander the Great. Conservative scholars with few exceptions generally identify the fourth beast as Rome.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV12 — Interpretation

quote.text: The interpretation of chapter 2 is confirmed by chapter 7, which has special revelation concerning the fourth empire in its yet future stage

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV82 — The Interpretation of the Vision of the Fourth Beast

quote.text: If the ten kings are in power at the end of the age, which also seems to be supported by the ten kings of Revelation 13:1; 17:12, it follows that they must be still future. The fact that they appear in the book of Revelation, written long after the fall of the Grecian Empire, plainly relates them to the Roman Empire in its final stage.

positions.position: Future personal Antichrist, the final Roman-empire world ruler; Antiochus IV Epiphanes functions only as typological foreshadowing, not fulfillment.
positions.rationale: Walvoord rejects the critical identification of the little horn with Antiochus IV because the complete fulfillment will be 'much more severe and extensive.' He permits Antiochus as foreshadowing but insists the consummation — three-and-a-half years of persecution of the saints, destruction at the second advent — is future. He maps the Daniel 7 little horn onto the beast of Revelation 13/17 and the king of Daniel 11:36ff.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV82 — The Interpretation of the Vision of the Fourth Beast

quote.text: Just as there is special emphasis upon the fourth beast in the vision, so in the prophetic interpretation particular attention is given to the little horn, the outstanding personage at the end of the age, who will be destroyed with the inauguration of the kingdom from heaven.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV82 — The Interpretation of the Vision of the Fourth Beast

quote.text: Critics relate this to Antiochus Epiphanes.83 While Antiochus may foreshadow the activities of the little horn of Daniel 7, the complete fulfillment will be much more severe and extensive.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV128 — The King of the End Time

quote.text: A better identification of the king, the second identification, however, is to relate him to the Roman world ruler, the same individual as the little horn of Daniel 7 and the beast out of the sea of Revelation 13:1–10.

positions.position: Literal year-weeks with a gap between the sixty-ninth and seventieth week; the seventieth week is a future seven-year period in which the Antichrist confirms a covenant and then breaks it at the midpoint.
positions.rationale: Walvoord argues the dividing line between views is verse 27: only the futurist 'gap' reading gives the prophecy a literal fulfillment. The 'he' of 9:27 goes back to 'the prince that shall come' of 9:26, identified with the future Antichrist (not Christ, not Antiochus, not Titus). The 'time, times, and half a time' of Dan 7:25, 12:7 and the forty-two months of Revelation 11-13 all cross-confirm a three-and-a-half-year second half of the seventieth week.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV104 — The Seventieth Seven

quote.text: Here the choice is clearly between literal fulfillment, which requires a futuristic interpretation with a gap between the sixty-ninth and seventieth week, or several other options which admittedly do not provide any clear fulfillment of verse 27.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV104 — The Seventieth Seven

quote.text: If the normal rule be followed that the antecedent is the nearest preceding possibility, it would go back to the prince that shall come of verse 26. This is the normal premillennial interpretation which postulates that the reference is to a future prince who may be identified with the Antichrist

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV82 — The Interpretation of the Vision of the Fourth Beast

quote.text: the meaning seems clearly to refer to the last three and one-half years preceding the second advent of Christ, which will bring in the final form of the kingdom of God on earth. The three and one-half year computation is confirmed by the forty-two months, or three and one-half years, in Revelation 11:2 and 13:5

positions.position: Dispensational-futurist. Daniel's apocalyptic visions are symbolic-yet-literal predictive prophecy; Revelation 6-19 is the extended parallel to Daniel's end-time material.
positions.rationale: Walvoord affirms Daniel's apocalyptic genre (with Alexander's definition) but sharply distinguishes biblical apocalyptic from pseudepigraphal apocalyptic, insisting divine interpretation within the vision secures a determinate meaning. Revelation 6-19 is treated as a parallel extension of Daniel's end-time revelation; Daniel 7 is a 'major outline' to which Revelation adds detail.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV7 — Apocalyptical Character

quote.text: The fact that a book is apocalyptic does not necessarily mean that its revelation is obscure or uncertain, and conservative scholarship has recognized the legitimacy of apocalyptic revelation as a genuine means of divine communication.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV71 — 7 Daniel’s Vision Of Future World History

quote.text: Interpreted in this way, the chapter forms a major outline of future events to which additional details are given later in the book of Daniel and in the New Testament, especially in the Revelation.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV133 — The Great Tribulation

quote.text: This is made clear from a further study of Revelation 6–19, where the great catastrophies which overtake the world in the breaking of the seals, the blowing of the trumpets, and the emptying of the vials of divine judgment decimate the world’s population.

positions.position: Premillennial. The kingdom of the Son of Man is a future, political, worldwide messianic kingdom established at Christ's second advent, not the present spiritual church-age reign.
positions.rationale: Walvoord explicitly identifies his book as filling a gap because prior Daniel commentaries (Leupold, Young) were amillennial. He argues the fifth kingdom of Daniel 2 and 7 is not the present church but a future political kingdom: the interpretation of Dan 7:23-27 'demands that, for the fifth kingdom to overcome the fourth, the fifth must be basically a sovereign and political kingdom.' The Son of Man's reign corresponds to the millennial passages like Psalm 2 and Isaiah 11.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV2 — Preface

quote.text: Among all these works, however, no thorough commentary from the pre-millennial conservative point of view has appeared. The important works of H. C. Leupold and Edward Young, standard commentaries on Daniel, present only the amillennial view

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV82 — The Interpretation of the Vision of the Fourth Beast

quote.text: the interpretation of verses 23–27 demands that, for the fifth kingdom to overcome the fourth, the fifth must be basically a sovereign and political kingdom, whatever its spiritual characteristics. By so much, it also demands that this be a future fulfillment, inasmuch as nothing in history corresponds to this.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV79 — The Fifth Kingdom of the Son of Man from Heaven

quote.text: The description of Him as being worthy of ruling all nations is obviously in keeping with many passages in the Bible referring to the millennial rule of Jesus Christ, as for instance, Psalm 2:6–9 and Isaiah 11.

positions.position: Consistent futurist premillennialism with a sharp distinction between Israel and the nations. Daniel 11:36ff, Daniel 7's fourth-beast climax, Daniel 9:27, and Daniel 12:1 all refer to a single future 'time of the end' culminating in Christ's second advent.
positions.rationale: Walvoord organizes Daniel around three prophetic programs (nations, Israel, church) and reads the book's final visions as converging on one future end-time crisis. The passage in Dan 11:36-45, the tribulation of Dan 12:1, the destruction of the little horn of Dan 7, and the seventieth week of Dan 9:27 are all 'contemporaneous' — the climax of the interadvent age.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV2 — Preface

quote.text: Of the three prophetic programs revealed in Scripture, outlining the course of the nations, Israel, and the church, Daniel alone reveals the details of God’s plan for both the nations and Israel.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV128 — The King of the End Time

quote.text: The passage, therefore, is to be considered as contemporaneous with the climax of chapter 2, the destruction of the image, and the destruction of the little horn of Daniel 7, a period described in the book of Revelation, chapters 6–19.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV133 — The Great Tribulation

quote.text: This description of the time of the end confirms Daniel’s revelation that the time of the end will be a period of trouble such as the world has never known

positions.position: Messianic — a personal, divine, individual figure (Jesus Christ), distinct from God the Father (the Ancient of Days) and distinct from the saints; the collective-Israel and angel interpretations are rejected.
positions.rationale: Walvoord holds that the Son of Man is 'a picture of the Lord Jesus Christ rather than an angelic agency' and rejects Montgomery's and (later) the critical-modern view that this figure personifies Israel or the saints. His argument is cumulative: the 'clouds of heaven' imply deity; the New Testament Christological use of 'Son of Man' is the 'divine commentary'; a personification of the saints would be tautological since the saints are already the beneficiaries.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV79 — The Fifth Kingdom of the Son of Man from Heaven

quote.text: Conservative scholars are agreed that the Son of man is a picture of the Lord Jesus Christ rather than an angelic agency.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV79 — The Fifth Kingdom of the Son of Man from Heaven

quote.text: Only Christ will come with clouds of heaven, and be the King of kings and Lord of lords over all nations throughout eternity. Inasmuch as all the nations which survive His purging judgment and come under His dominion are saints, it would be tautology to make the Son of man the personification of the saints.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV77 — The Vision of the Ancient of Days

quote.text: The Ancient of days seems to correspond to God the Father, as distinct from God the Son who is introduced in Daniel 7:13 as Son of man.

positions.position: Classical dispensationalism: three distinct prophetic programs (nations, Israel, church); Daniel speaks to the nations and Israel, not the church; Israel's national promises await literal future fulfillment.
positions.rationale: Walvoord frames Daniel at the outset as addressing two of three distinct prophetic programs — the nations and Israel — and insists the deliverance of Dan 12:1 refers to ethnic-national Israel, 'rather than to all the saints as Young and Leupold interpret it.' He maps a 'times of the Gentiles' / Israel dichotomy throughout, preserves a future for national Israel, and identifies chapters 8-12 as 'Israel in relation to the Gentiles' (following Culver/Auberlen).

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV2 — Preface

quote.text: Of the three prophetic programs revealed in Scripture, outlining the course of the nations, Israel, and the church, Daniel alone reveals the details of God’s plan for both the nations and Israel.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV133 — The Great Tribulation

quote.text: The repeated reference to “thy people,” twice in one verse, seems to limit this to the people of Israel, rather than to all the saints as Young and Leupold interpret it, after Calvin.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV72 — Daniel’s First Vision: The Four Great Beasts

quote.text: Culver’s point of view, which he credits to Auberlen,14 has much to commend itself and is especially theologically discerning because it distinguishes the two major programs of God in the Old Testament, namely, the program for the Gentiles and the program for Israel.

positions.position: Literal-historical-predictive (what Walvoord calls interpreting 'in its normal sense'), while recognizing the apocalyptic symbolic mode; symbols are decoded via in-text divine interpretation and cross-reference with later biblical revelation.
positions.rationale: Walvoord's programmatic statement in the preface is that he interprets 'prophecy in its normal sense while, at the same time, recognizing the apocalyptic character of its revelation.' His operating principle is that the text yields determinate meaning when the reader admits predictive prophecy, attends to in-vision interpretation, and correlates later fulfillment in history (for the already-fulfilled) and later Scripture (for the still-future).

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV2 — Preface

quote.text: In attempting an interpretation of the book of Daniel, the principle has been followed of interpreting prophecy in its normal sense while, at the same time, recognizing the apocalyptic character of its revelation.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV12 — Interpretation

quote.text: If the premise be granted that the book of Daniel is genuine Scripture and that detailed prediction of the future as in Daniel may be admitted as genuine, the problems of interpretation are then reduced to determining what the text actually says.

positions.position: Tight integration of Daniel 2, Daniel 7, Daniel 9, Daniel 11-12, Matthew 24, 2 Thessalonians 2, and Revelation 11-13, 17. The same figure (Antichrist / little horn / king / beast) and the same three-and-a-half-year period appear across the canon.
positions.rationale: Walvoord repeatedly maps Daniel 7's imagery onto Revelation: the throne scene corresponds to Revelation 4-5, the ten horns to Revelation 13:1 and 17:12, the three-and-a-half years to Revelation 11-13. He further identifies the king of Daniel 11:36 with the man of sin of 2 Thessalonians 2 and the beast of Revelation 13. Christ's citation of Daniel in the Olivet Discourse and Revelation 13's parallel are cited as decisive against a second-century exhaustive fulfillment.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV77 — The Vision of the Ancient of Days

quote.text: The scene as a whole corresponds to what John saw and recorded in Revelation 4–5. The Ancient of days seems to correspond to God the Father, as distinct from God the Son who is introduced in Daniel 7:13 as Son of man.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV71 — 7 Daniel’s Vision Of Future World History

quote.text: Prophecies of the book of Revelation written late in the first century also anticipate as future the fulfillment of parallel prophecies in Daniel. For example, Revelation 13 parallels the final stage of Daniel’s fourth empire.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV128 — The King of the End Time

quote.text: A better identification of the king, the second identification, however, is to relate him to the Roman world ruler, the same individual as the little horn of Daniel 7 and the beast out of the sea of Revelation 13:1–10.

positions.position: Single-fulfillment futurism for the end-time details (ten horns, little horn, seventieth week), with typological foreshadowing permitted for Antiochus IV. Earlier chapters/verses are treated as single past fulfillments (Medo-Persia, Greece, 69 weeks to Messiah).
positions.rationale: Walvoord's structure is past fulfillment for chapters 2-6, 8, 11:1-35 and the first sixty-nine weeks; single future fulfillment for Dan 7:7-27 from the ten horns on, Dan 9:27, Dan 11:36-45, Dan 12:1. Antiochus IV 'may foreshadow' the little horn but is not a partial-fulfillment in a dual-horizon sense — the critical-modern/Antiochus reading is rejected outright, not incorporated.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV79 — The Fifth Kingdom of the Son of Man from Heaven

quote.text: third, they could find these prophecies to be distinctly future and not at all fulfilled by the first coming of Christ, the decline of the Roman Empire, or that which is historic. The third view, which is the futuristic interpretation, is the only one which provides the possibility of literal fulfillment of this prophecy.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV128 — The King of the End Time

quote.text: Beginning with verse 36, however, an entirely different situation obtains. No commentator claims to find precise fulfillment in the remainder of this chapter.

crossBookReadings.positionSummary: Walvoord treats Revelation 13 (beast from the sea with ten horns) and Revelation 17:12 (ten kings) as direct extensions of Daniel 7's fourth-beast prophecy. Because Revelation is written long after Greece has fallen, Revelation's ten-king imagery locks the Daniel 7 ten horns to the final Roman stage. The beast of Revelation 13:1-10 is the same individual as Daniel's little horn and the king of Daniel 11:36.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV82 — The Interpretation of the Vision of the Fourth Beast

quote.text: If the ten kings are in power at the end of the age, which also seems to be supported by the ten kings of Revelation 13:1; 17:12, it follows that they must be still future.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV128 — The King of the End Time

quote.text: A better identification of the king, the second identification, however, is to relate him to the Roman world ruler, the same individual as the little horn of Daniel 7 and the beast out of the sea of Revelation 13:1–10.

crossBookReadings.positionSummary: Walvoord cites Christ's reference to 'Daniel the prophet' in Matthew 24:15 as both (a) dominical endorsement of sixth-century Daniel and (b) a future-oriented reading: Christ still anticipates the abomination of desolation (Dan 9:27; 12:11) as future from his own standpoint, ruling out an exhaustive second-century fulfillment.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV71 — 7 Daniel’s Vision Of Future World History

quote.text: Christ Himself in Matthew 24:15 predicted the abomination of desolation of Daniel 12:11 as future, not past.

frontend.author: Walvoord
frontend.title: Daniel: The Key to Prophetic Revelation
frontend.section: §DIV133 — The Great Tribulation

quote.text: Christ described the great tribulation as beginning with “the abomination of desolation, spoken of by Daniel the prophet” (Mt 24:15), a reference to the breaking of the covenant and desecration of the temple in Daniel 9:27.

distinctiveMoves: Diagnoses the interpretive divide on the fourth kingdom as fundamentally an argument about whether predictive prophecy is possible, not an argument about exegetical detail — Roman identification follows from supernaturalism, Greek identification follows from the Maccabean-forgery thesis.

Splits Daniel 7 temporally: verses 1-6 describe historical Babylon/Medo-Persia/Greece, but from 'it had ten horns' in verse 7 through verse 14 the prophecy is still future. This yields a 'past-then-future' reading of a single continuous vision.

Admits Antiochus IV as typological foreshadowing of the little horn but explicitly denies that his career fulfills the prophecy — Antiochus is a type, the Antichrist is the antitype, and the two are not collapsed.

Treats the three-and-a-half-year periods across Daniel 7:25, 9:27, 12:7, and Revelation 11-13 as one calendar-identical period, functioning as a canonical knot that locks Daniel's end-time and Revelation's end-time into a single sequence.

Frames his commentary project as a deliberate response to the amillennial dominance of Leupold/Young and the liberal dominance of Montgomery/Driver — writing from Dallas Seminary as a consciously premillennial-dispensational answer.

Distinguishes the Ancient of Days and the Son of Man as two distinct divine persons in a binitarian-looking reading, against Gaebelein's conflation of the two, using Dan 7:13's 'brought before him' as dispositive.
uncertainties: Walvoord rarely hedges. Where he registers options, he almost always declares a 'better' or 'preferable' view rather than leaving the question open.

On whether the ten horns are ten nations or ten individual kings, he follows the kings reading but does not extensively engage with the nation-state reading popular in later dispensationalism (Common Market / EU speculation absent from this 1971 text).

The identity of Darius the Mede (Dan 5:31; 9:1) is not directly in our Daniel-7 pilot axes but relates to dating arguments; Walvoord leans on Whitcomb's monograph rather than defending a position himself.

Son of Man: Walvoord is clear that the figure is Christ and is distinct from the saints, but does not fully develop the Israel/church distinction at Dan 7:13-14 the way later dispensationalists have — the 'saints of the Most High' of 7:18, 27 are treated as the beneficiaries of Christ's reign without explicit Israel/church partitioning.
